103	  \label{thm:some_label}

127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. 2	\label{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-184). This chapter is the A.3 sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It builds the
7	% identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of the Picard scheme as an
8	% open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\), equips it with the
9	% degree-zero characterisation, and identifies it as an abelian variety
10	% of dimension \(g(C)\). The abstract identity-component substrate
11	% (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme} +
12	% \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is new project material:
13	% the Lean target
14	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} does not yet
15	% exist in Mathlib.
16	%
17	% NOTE: future split candidate when a second consumer materialises. The
18	% abstract identity-component substrate is kept in this chapter (rather
19	% than split off into a separate
20	% \texttt{GroupScheme\_IdentityComponent.tex}) because it is small and
21	% has a single consumer for now. If a second consumer materialises,
22	% revisit the split.
23	
24	% NOTE (planner directive iter-184). The Lean target file
25	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean} does not yet
26	% exist; this chapter is the authoritative specification, and the Lean
27	% skeleton is owed in a follow-up iteration.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}
94	
95	\begin{theorem}\leanok
96	[Identity component is an open subgroup scheme of finite type]
97	  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
98	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
99	  
100	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
101	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
102	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
103	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
104	  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
105	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
106	  % extending \(k\)."
107	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
108	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
109	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
110	  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
111	  subscheme of \(G\), the open immersion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a
112	  homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes (in particular \(G^0\) inherits a
113	  \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\)), \(G^0\) is of finite type over
114	  \(k\) and geometrically irreducible, and the formation of \(G^0\)
115	  commutes with extension of the base field: for any field extension
116	  \(K/k\), the natural map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is an isomorphism.
117	\end{theorem}
118	
119	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
120	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
121	% (3).)
122	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
123	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
124	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
125	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
126	% \(G^0\) is open too.
127	%
128	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
129	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
130	% extending \(k\).
131	%
132	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
133	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
134	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
135	%
136	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
137	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
138	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
139	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
140	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
141	% [\dots]
142	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
143	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
144	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
145	% [\dots]
146	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
147	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
148	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
149	\begin{proof}
150	  
151	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
152	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
153	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
154	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
155	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
156	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
157	  the open-subscheme structure.
158	
159	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
160	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
161	  particular the formation of \(G^0\) commutes with extension of the
162	  base field. The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is again connected
163	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
164	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
165	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
166	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group operation
167	  (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
168	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes.
169	
170	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
171	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
172	  commutes with field extension). Over \(\overline k\), the product
173	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
174	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
175	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
176	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
177	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
178	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
179	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
180	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
181	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
182	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
183	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
184	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
185	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
186	\end{proof}

232	\section{The degree map}
233	\label{sec:pic_degree}
234	
235	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
236	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
237	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
238	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
239	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
240	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
241	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
242	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
243	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
244	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
245	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
246	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
247	degree.
248	
249	\begin{definition}\leanok
250	[Degree of a Picard class]
251	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
253	  
254	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
255	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
256	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
257	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
258	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
259	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
260	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
261	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
262	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
263	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
264	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
265	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
266	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
267	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
268	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
269	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
270	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
271	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
272	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
273	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
274	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
275	  \[
276	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
277	  \]
278	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
279	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
280	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
281	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
282	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
283	  \[
284	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
285	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
286	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
287	  \]
288	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
289	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
290	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
291	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
292	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
293	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
294	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
295	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
296	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
297	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
298	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
299	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
300	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
301	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
302	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
303	  of degree \(d\).
304	\end{definition}

306	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
307	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
308	
309	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
310	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
311	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
312	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
313	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
314	automatically.
315	
316	\begin{theorem}\leanok
317	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety of dimension \(g\)]
318	  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
319	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
320	  
321	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
322	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
323	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
324	  % L3990-L3996).
325	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
326	  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
327	  % Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
328	  % curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Show the
329	  % ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
330	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
331	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth.
332	  % \end{ex}"
333	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
334	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
335	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
336	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
337	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
338	  % Exercise~REF."
339	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
340	  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
341	  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8) and Rmk.~III.1.4(e),
342	  p.~86 (dimension equals genus).}
343	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
344	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). Then the identity component
345	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an
346	  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)}: a smooth, proper, geometrically
347	  irreducible commutative \(k\)-group scheme. Its dimension is
348	  \[
349	    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g \;=\; g(C),
350	  \]
351	  the genus of \(C\) (\cref{def:genus}). Its \(k\)-points are exactly the
352	  classes of invertible sheaves of degree zero:
353	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\), the kernel of the degree map of
354	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.
355	\end{theorem}
356	
357	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
358	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
359	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
360	% identity-component substrate.)
361	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
362	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
363	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
364	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
365	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
366	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
367	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
368	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
369	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
370	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
371	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
372	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
373	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
374	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
375	% everywhere."
376	% (from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86:)
377	% "(e) The dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\). If \(C\) has genus zero,
378	% then \(\Jac(C) = 0\) (this is obvious, because \(\Pic^0(C) = 0\), even
379	% when one goes to the algebraic closure). If \(C\) has genus 1, then
380	% \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs
381	% from \(C\) --- because \(\Jac(C)\) always has a point)."
382	\begin{proof}
383	  
384	  We assemble the conclusion from four inputs.
385	
386	  \emph{Open and closed subgroup of finite type, geometrically
387	  irreducible.} By
388	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
389	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
390	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
391	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
392	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
393	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
394	  separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
395	  and connected: it is a complete subscheme \emph{candidate} for the
396	  abelian-variety conclusion.
397	
398	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
399	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
400	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
401	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
402	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
403	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
404	
405	  \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper geometrically
406	  integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is also geometrically
407	  normal (the smoothness implies geometric regularity, hence geometric
408	  normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p then upgrades the
409	  quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
410	  projective over \(k\), in particular proper. The proof of th:qpp\&p
411	  uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem to reduce to ruling
412	  out non-constant maps \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a
413	  geometrically normal \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
414	
415	  \emph{Smoothness and dimension.} By Kleiman~\S 5, Cor.~cor:sm,
416	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g\)
417	  (\cref{def:genus}), with equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is
418	  smooth at \(0\), in which case it is smooth of dimension \(g\)
419	  everywhere. For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the Picard scheme is
420	  smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of Kleiman~\S 5, which
421	  asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat with integral geometric
422	  fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the generalised Jacobians
423	  \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth quasi-projective family of relative
424	  dimension \(p_a\); specialising to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth
425	  proper curve of (geometric = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives
426	  smoothness and dimension \(g\) for \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
427	
428	  Combining all four steps: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
429	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\). A
430	  proper connected \(k\)-group variety is by definition an
431	  \emph{abelian variety} (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically
432	  commutative (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an
433	  abelian variety of dimension \(g\).
434	
435	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map.} A class
436	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of the
437	  identity precisely when the Hilbert polynomial of a representing
438	  invertible sheaf coincides with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\) ---
439	  equivalently, when its degree (the leading coefficient of the
440	  Hilbert polynomial of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
441	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb Z)\).
442	\end{proof}

201	
202	The disjoint-union structure of `Pic_{C/k}` (a disjoint union of open
203	quasi-projective `k`-subschemes, indexed by Hilbert polynomial via

\begin{theorem}\leanok
[Identity component is an open subgroup scheme of finite type]
  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
  
  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
  % extending \(k\)."
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
  subscheme of \(G\), the open immersion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a
  homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes (in particular \(G^0\) inherits a
  \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\)), \(G^0\) is of finite type over
  \(k\) and geometrically irreducible, and the formation of \(G^0\)
  commutes with extension of the base field: for any field extension
  \(K/k\), the natural map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is an isomorphism.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
% (3).)
% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
% \(G^0\) is open too.
%
% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
% extending \(k\).
%
% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
%
% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
% [\dots]
% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
% [\dots]
% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
\begin{proof}
  
  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
  the open-subscheme structure.

  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
  particular the formation of \(G^0\) commutes with extension of the
  base field. The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is again connected
  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group operation
  (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes.

  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
  commutes with field extension). Over \(\overline k\), the product
  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}\leanok
[Identity component is a clopen subscheme]
  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}

  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
  % extending \(k\)."
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
  subscheme of \(G\): the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is both an
  open immersion and a closed immersion of schemes.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}
[Identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism]
  \label{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}

  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
  type. The clopen inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\)
  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is a homomorphism of
  \(k\)-group schemes: \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure
  from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over
  \(\Spec k\) is compatible with the group laws on source and target.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}
[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]
  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}

  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}
  quasi-compact --- and geometrically irreducible: after base change
  to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the underlying scheme
  \(G^0_{\bar k}\) is an irreducible \(\bar k\)-scheme.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}
[Formation of the identity component commutes with base change]
  \label{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}

  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
  Let \(k\) be a field, \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
  type, and \(K/k\) a field extension. The natural map
  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\), induced by base-changing the inclusion of
  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} along \(\Spec K \to \Spec k\)
  and using the universal property of the identity component on
  \(G_K\), is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
% (3).)
% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
% \(G^0\) is open too.
%
% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
% extending \(k\).
%
% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
%
% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
% [\dots]
% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
% [\dots]
% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
\begin{proof}
  We establish
  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).

  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.

  \emph{Base-change commutation
  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.

  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).

  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
  \(\overline k\), the product
  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}\leanok
[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety of dimension \(g\)]
  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
  
  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
  % L3990-L3996).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
  % Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
  % curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Show the
  % ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth.
  % \end{ex}"
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
  % Exercise~REF."
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8) and Rmk.~III.1.4(e),
  p.~86 (dimension equals genus).}
  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
  \(g\) over a field \(k\). Then the identity component
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an
  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)}: a smooth, proper, geometrically
  irreducible commutative \(k\)-group scheme. Its dimension is
  \[
    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g \;=\; g(C),
  \]
  the genus of \(C\) (\cref{def:genus}). Its \(k\)-points are exactly the
  classes of invertible sheaves of degree zero:
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\), the kernel of the degree map of
  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}
[Dimension of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals the genus of \(C\)]
  \label{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}

  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
  % \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
  % PDF p.~92).

  % "(e) The dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\). If \(C\) has genus zero,

  % \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs
  % from \(C\) --- because \(\Jac(C)\) always has a point)."
  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
  \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86; cf.\ [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'',

\begin{theorem}
[\(k\)-points of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]
  \label{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}

  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at
  % \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} (read from
  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~94).
  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})
  if and only if the degree
  \(\deg(\lambda) \in \mathbb{Z}\) of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}
  vanishes. Equivalently,
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker\bigl(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\bigr)\).
\end{theorem}

529	  We assemble the conclusion from four inputs.
530	
531	  \emph{Open and closed subgroup of finite type, geometrically
532	  irreducible.} By
533	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
534	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
535	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
536	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
537	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
538	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
539	  separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
540	  and connected: it is a complete subscheme \emph{candidate} for the
541	  abelian-variety conclusion.
542	
543	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
544	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
545	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
546	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
547	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
548	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
549	
550	  \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper geometrically
551	  integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is also geometrically
552	  normal (the smoothness implies geometric regularity, hence geometric
553	  normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p then upgrades the
554	  quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
555	  projective over \(k\), in particular proper. The proof of th:qpp\&p
556	  uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem to reduce to ruling
557	  out non-constant maps \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a
558	  geometrically normal \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
559	
560	  \emph{Smoothness and dimension.} By Kleiman~\S 5, Cor.~cor:sm,
561	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g\)
562	  (\cref{def:genus}), with equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is
563	  smooth at \(0\), in which case it is smooth of dimension \(g\)
564	  everywhere. For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the Picard scheme is
565	  smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of Kleiman~\S 5, which
566	  asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat with integral geometric
567	  fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the generalised Jacobians
568	  \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth quasi-projective family of relative
569	  dimension \(p_a\); specialising to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth
570	  proper curve of (geometric = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives
571	  smoothness and dimension \(g\) for \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
572	
573	  Combining all four steps: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
574	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\). A
575	  proper connected \(k\)-group variety is by definition an
576	  \emph{abelian variety} (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically
577	  commutative (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an
578	  abelian variety of dimension \(g\).
579	
580	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map.} A class
581	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of the
582	  identity precisely when the Hilbert polynomial of a representing
583	  invertible sheaf coincides with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\) ---
584	  equivalently, when its degree (the leading coefficient of the
585	  Hilbert polynomial of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
586	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb Z)\).
587	\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  
  We assemble the conclusion from four inputs.

  \emph{Open and closed subgroup of finite type, geometrically
  irreducible.} By
  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
  separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
  and connected: it is a complete subscheme \emph{candidate} for the
  abelian-variety conclusion.

  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).

  \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper geometrically
  integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is also geometrically
  normal (the smoothness implies geometric regularity, hence geometric
  normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p then upgrades the
  quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
  projective over \(k\), in particular proper. The proof of th:qpp\&p
  uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem to reduce to ruling
  out non-constant maps \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a
  geometrically normal \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.

  \emph{Smoothness and dimension.} By Kleiman~\S 5, Cor.~cor:sm,
  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g\)
  (\cref{def:genus}), with equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is
  smooth at \(0\), in which case it is smooth of dimension \(g\)
  everywhere. For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the Picard scheme is
  smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of Kleiman~\S 5, which
  asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat with integral geometric
  fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the generalised Jacobians
  \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth quasi-projective family of relative
  dimension \(p_a\); specialising to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth
  proper curve of (geometric = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives
  smoothness and dimension \(g\) for \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).

  Combining all four steps: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\). A
  proper connected \(k\)-group variety is by definition an
  \emph{abelian variety} (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically
  commutative (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an
  abelian variety of dimension \(g\).

  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of the
  identity precisely when the Hilbert polynomial of a representing
  invertible sheaf coincides with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\) ---
  equivalently, when its degree (the leading coefficient of the
  Hilbert polynomial of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence

\begin{proof}
  We establish
  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).

  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
  conjuncts from four inputs.

  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
  By
  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
  type by
  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.

  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).

  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.

  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).

  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.

  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
  the open identity component, and
  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.

  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
  zero on \(C\)'').
\end{proof}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
    (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is a structural
    constant naming the open-and-closed identity-component subscheme
    of a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type. The construction
    is the abstract substrate of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps,
    independent of the Picard context; it is new project material
    (\emph{not} yet in Mathlib).
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
    (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) packages the
    clopen-subscheme inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) (open immersion
    \emph{and} closed immersion) --- conclusion (a) of Kleiman~\S 5
    Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
    (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}) packages
    the group-homomorphism property of the inclusion of
    \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}: \(G^0\) inherits a
    \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\) compatible with the
    inclusion. Conclusion (b) of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
    (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible})
    packages the finite-type-ness and geometric-irreducibility
    conclusions of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (conclusion (c)).
    In Lean these are the instances
    \texttt{[LocallyOfFiniteType (IdentityComponent G).hom]},
    \texttt{[QuasiCompact (IdentityComponent G).hom]}, and
    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible (IdentityComponent G).hom]}.
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
    (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}) packages the
    base-change-commutation conclusion (d) of Kleiman~\S 5
    Lem.~lem:agps~(3): for any field extension \(K/k\), the natural
    map \((\IdentityComponent G)_K \to \IdentityComponent (G_K)\) is
    an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
    (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is the open subscheme of
    \(\Pic_{C/k}\) obtained by applying the
    \texttt{IdentityComponent} substrate to
    \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), inheriting the \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
    \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}.
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
    (\cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is the group homomorphism
    \(\Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\) extracting the leading coefficient
    of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
    relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on
    \(C\). On the disjoint-union decomposition of
    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} the degree is the index of the
    component.
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) is the bundled
    abelian-variety structure on \texttt{Pic0Scheme}: the four-conjunct
    conjunction \([\IsProper]\), \([\Smooth]\),
    \([\GeometricallyIrreducible]\) on the structural morphism
    \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with
    \texttt{Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))}.
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}) is the dimension
    formula \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\) (Milne~\S III.1,
    Rmk.~1.4(e)). In Lean this is the equation
    \texttt{Module.finrank k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C}
    (precise statement via the project's Krull-dimension API on the
    underlying scheme).
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}) is the
    \(k\)-point-characterisation
    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\) (Milne~\S III.1, p.~88). In Lean,
    \texttt{∀ λ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left,
    (∃ μ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left,\,
    μ ≫ pic0Inclusion = λ) ↔ PicScheme.degree C λ = 0}.
\end{itemize}

The abstract identity-component substrate
(\texttt{GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} and its four
conclusion-specific theorems
\texttt{IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme},
\texttt{IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism},
\texttt{IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible},
\texttt{IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}) is new project material: it
is not currently in Mathlib, and the Lean skeleton is owed in a
follow-up iteration. The downstream
\texttt{Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety},
\texttt{Pic0Scheme.finrank\_eq\_genus}, and
\texttt{Pic0Scheme.kPoints\_iff\_kerDegree} consume Kleiman~\S 5
Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac and Milne~\S III.1, both of which currently
live only at the blueprint level; their Lean specialisation to the
smooth-proper-curve case is the main substantive step of A.3.
